title: Diving Deeper into 'Running on the Roof of the World'
simple_explanation: This page is like being a story detective and then a story creator! First, you answer questions about the story, finding clues about words and feelings. Then, you get to write your own part of the story, making up what happens next!
detailed_explanation: You'll need to find examples of figurative language, like 'personification' (giving human actions to things) and 'simile' (comparing things using 'like' or 'as'), right in the story. You'll also practice figuring out what new words mean by looking at the words around them. This is called using 'context clues' and it's super helpful when you read. Then, the page asks you to get creative! You'll write your own paragraph to continue the story. You get to decide what happens next with Tash, Sam, and the soldiers. When you write, remember to include exciting descriptions, dialogue (what characters say), and different kinds of sentences to make your story flow well and keep the reader hooked.

examples_expanded.text: For personification, you might find how the wind 'bites' – wind doesn't really bite like a person or animal.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of analyzing fiction: comprehension & writing in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: For a simile, 'soar like the golden eagles' tells you Tash flies really high and freely, just like an eagle.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of analyzing fiction: comprehension & writing in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: To understand 'ultimate defeat', the sentence 'Falling over now would be the ultimate defeat' shows it means the worst possible thing that could happen, a really bad loss.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of analyzing fiction: comprehension & writing in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: When you continue the story, you could write dialogue like: ''Run!' whispered Sam, pulling Tash up quickly.' This uses speech marks and shows their urgency.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of analyzing fiction: comprehension & writing in a real sentence.

guided_practice: Identify the meaning of 'personification' in a sentence. Remember it means: Giving human qualities or actions to objects or animals.
Match the word 'simile' with a similar concept from your own experience.
Look at the first example again. How would you modify it to make it your own?
application_tasks: Create 3 of your own sentences using the core idea of Analyzing Fiction: Comprehension & Writing.
Imagine you are teaching this topic to a friend. Write down exactly what you would say.
challenge_tasks: Combine the concept of 'personification' with something you learned in a previous chapter to write a short paragraph.
Can you find an exception to this rule or a creative way to bend it? Explain how.
key_points: Practice finding figurative language in texts.
Use surrounding words (context) to understand new vocabulary.
Analyze characters' feelings and motivations in a story.
Develop creative writing skills by continuing a narrative.
Remember to use varied sentence structures and correct punctuation in your writing.

vocabulary.definition: Giving human qualities or actions to objects or animals.
Compares two different things using 'like' or 'as'.
A section of writing, usually made of several sentences, that deals with one main idea.
A small part or section taken from a larger text, like a book.
The time and place where a story happens.
The words, phrases, or sentences that come before and after a particular word or phrase, helping to explain its meaning.
The last, final, or most extreme of its kind.
The act of losing a battle, game, or competition.
An idea, feeling, or opinion about something or someone.
Words that describe nouns (people, places, things).
Wanting to win or be more successful than others.
Willing to follow rules or do what others say.
Feeling or showing fear; scared.
Feeling embarrassed or guilty.
Talking about yourself or your achievements with too much pride.
Exact, accurate, and careful.
A group of words that acts like an adverb, telling us how, when, where, or why something happened.
A sentence made of two simple sentences joined by words like 'and', 'but', or 'or'.
A sentence that contains one main idea and one or more ideas that add extra information.
The marks, such as commas and full stops, used in writing to separate sentences and parts of sentences.
A punctuation mark (;) used to join two closely related independent clauses or to separate items in a complex list.
A punctuation mark (:) used to introduce a list, an explanation, or a quotation.
Punctuation marks (" ") used to show exactly what someone has said.
